heroOverview: Anhidrosis is the inability to sweat normally, a functional failure of the eccrine sweat glands that removes one of the body's primary heat-dissipation mechanisms and creates a serious risk of heat exhaustion and life-threatening heatstroke during physical activity or hot weather. It may be generalised — affecting the entire body surface — or localised to one region, and it can arise from damage anywhere along the sweat pathway: from neural control centres and peripheral autonomic nerves, to the sweat gland itself. Acquired generalised anhidrosis can represent the first clinically detectable sign of diabetic autonomic neuropathy or primary Sjögren syndrome, among other systemic diseases. Recognition is clinically important because a patient who cannot sweat in a warm environment may appear well until a rapidly developing hyperthermia emergency occurs.

keyStats.prevalence: True prevalence of anhidrosis is unknown; it is considered rare as an isolated diagnosis but common as a feature of diabetic autonomic neuropathy, which affects up to 60% of people with longstanding diabetes (American Diabetes Association, 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; congenital forms present in infancy; acquired forms peak in middle-aged and older adults with underlying systemic disease
keyStats.avgAge: Acquired forms most common in adults over 40 with diabetes, Parkinson disease, or connective tissue disorders
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global figures unavailable; anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (a congenital cause) affects approximately 1 in 100,000 births worldwide

definition: Anhidrosis (ICD-10: L74.4) is defined as the complete or near-complete absence of sweating, which distinguishes it from hypohidrosis (reduced but not absent sweating). Because the eccrine sweat glands are the main effectors of evaporative cooling in humans, loss of their function impairs thermoregulation and predisposes to hyperthermia. Sweating is controlled by a cholinergic sympathetic pathway: the hypothalamic thermoregulatory centre detects rising core temperature and sends signals via the spinal cord to post-ganglionic sympathetic fibres that release acetylcholine onto M3 muscarinic receptors on eccrine gland cells. Anhidrosis can be caused by defects at any level — hypothalamic or cortical lesions, spinal cord injuries, peripheral neuropathies affecting post-ganglionic fibres, intrinsic eccrine gland destruction, or pharmacological blockade. It is classified as central (neurological lesion above the post-ganglionic neuron), peripheral (damage to post-ganglionic sympathetic fibres), or glandular (intrinsic eccrine gland abnormality or obstruction). Localised anhidrosis in a specific dermatomal or peripheral nerve distribution reflects a focal nerve injury; generalised anhidrosis points toward a systemic, metabolic, or widespread neurological cause. Anhidrosis falls primarily under dermatology (eccrine gland disorders, L74) but commonly prompts neurological and endocrinological co-management. It is distinct from hyperhidrosis (excessive sweating), which involves the same gland but in the opposite direction of dysfunction.

typesClassification.description: Affects the entire body surface, producing profound heat intolerance. Most often a consequence of widespread peripheral autonomic neuropathy (diabetic, autoimmune, or idiopathic), systemic drugs, or congenital ectodermal dysplasia.
Loss of sweating in a region corresponding to a spinal dermatome or peripheral nerve distribution, caused by spinal cord injury, radiculopathy, peripheral nerve injury, or post-herpetic anhidrosis.

Patchy loss of sweating without an identifiable cause, thought to reflect focal post-ganglionic sympathetic denervation. Often presents as heat intolerance in a circumscribed area discovered incidentally by the patient.
Anhidrosis confined to the ipsilateral face and neck alongside ptosis and miosis, reflecting interruption of the cervical sympathetic chain; the pattern identifies the anatomical level of the lesion.

Anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (Christ–Siemens–Touraine syndrome, X-linked) involves absent or markedly reduced eccrine glands from birth, combined with sparse hair and abnormal teeth. Presents in infancy with unexplained fevers.

primarySymptoms: Absence of visible sweating on warm skin or during exercise — often the defining observation that first alerts a patient or clinician to the problem.
Heat intolerance with rapid rise in body temperature during exercise or in warm environments, since evaporative cooling is absent or severely impaired.
Flushing and burning sensation of the skin as the body attempts compensatory vasodilation when sweating fails.
Headache and dizziness during heat exposure — early warning signs of developing hyperthermia when sweating is inadequate.
Fatigue and exercise intolerance disproportionate to the level of activity, driven by rising core temperature.

Compensatory hyperhidrosis in the areas that retain sweating — for example, profuse sweating on the face and head in patients with distal anhidrosis, as the body attempts to compensate through residual glands.
Dry, smooth skin in affected areas, lacking the moisture and texture that normal eccrine secretion provides.
Skin overheating and erythema localised to the anhidrotic zone during exertion.
In congenital anhidrosis, recurrent unexplained fever in infancy and childhood, often triggering repeated hospital admissions before the diagnosis is made.
Symptoms of the underlying cause — such as orthostatic dizziness in autonomic neuropathy, dry eyes and mouth in Sjögren syndrome, or tremor and rigidity in Parkinson disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing that one area of the body stays dry during vigorous exercise or heat exposure while the surrounding skin sweats normally.
Unusually high body temperature or prolonged recovery from heat exposure compared to others in the same environment.
Compensatory sweating becoming more pronounced on the face, scalp, or remaining body surface.
Heat intolerance that appears as a new symptom in someone with established diabetes, Parkinson disease, or Sjögren syndrome.
emergencySigns: Core body temperature above 40°C (104°F) with confusion, agitation, or loss of consciousness — heatstroke, a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate cooling and hospital admission.
Rapid heart rate, absence of sweating, and hot, dry, red skin in a hot environment — classic heatstroke triad requiring emergency services.
Seizures developing during physical activity in heat in a patient with known anhidrosis.
Anhidrosis developing suddenly over days in a systemically unwell patient — new onset in the context of acute illness may signal a paraneoplastic or autoimmune process requiring urgent investigation.

causes.description: Progressive glycation damage to post-ganglionic sympathetic cholinergic fibres innervating eccrine glands is one of the most common acquired causes of anhidrosis. The anhidrosis typically begins distally and ascends proximally in a length-dependent pattern; it affects up to 60% of patients with longstanding type 1 or type 2 diabetes and is associated with other autonomic dysfunction such as orthostatic hypotension and gastroparesis.

Autoimmune exocrinopathy that classically causes dry eyes and mouth but also destroys eccrine glands through a lymphocytic infiltrate targeting glandular epithelium; generalised anhidrosis in the absence of xerostomia can be the presenting feature. Anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies are the diagnostic markers.

A diagnosis of exclusion characterised by sudden-onset generalised anhidrosis in otherwise healthy young men, attributed to cholinergic urticaria or post-ganglionic cholinergic denervation on skin biopsy (reduced PGP9.5-immunopositive nerve fibres). Steroid treatment may induce remission in a subset.

Transection or progressive cavitation of the spinal cord interrupts descending thermoregulatory signals to the post-ganglionic sympathetic neurons below the level of the lesion, producing anhidrosis below the injury level; compensatory hyperhidrosis above the level is common.

causes.cause: Parkinson disease and multiple system atrophy
causes.description: Alpha-synuclein-driven degeneration of autonomic ganglia and peripheral sympathetic fibres in Parkinson disease causes patchy or generalised reduced sweating. Multiple system atrophy produces more severe and generalised anhidrosis due to central and peripheral autonomic degeneration.

X-linked hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (EDA gene mutation, Christ–Siemens–Touraine syndrome) results in aplasia or severe hypoplasia of eccrine glands from birth; affected boys cannot sweat and present with recurrent hyperthermia. Autosomal forms also exist.

Anticholinergic drugs (oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressants, antihistamines, scopolamine), botulinum toxin injections, and some antipsychotics block muscarinic acetylcholine receptors on eccrine glands, causing drug-induced anhidrosis. This is dose-dependent and usually reversible on drug discontinuation.

riskFactors.description: Duration of diabetes is the strongest predictor of autonomic neuropathy affecting sweat glands; each additional decade of diabetes approximately doubles the risk. Tight glycaemic control (HbA1c <53 mmol/mol / 7%) slows neuropathy progression.

riskFactors.factor: Primary Sjögren syndrome or other autoimmune disease
riskFactors.description: Systemic autoimmune conditions that involve exocrine glands — Sjögren syndrome, systemic lupus erythematosus, systemic sclerosis — can destroy eccrine glands through lymphocytic infiltration. Prevalence of anhidrosis in Sjögren syndrome is estimated at 3–10%.

riskFactors.factor: Parkinson disease or multiple system atrophy
riskFactors.description: Progressive central and peripheral autonomic degeneration in synucleinopathies invariably affects sweating regulation; up to 50% of Parkinson patients have clinically measurable sweat loss compared with age-matched controls.

Complete cervical or thoracic spinal cord injury above the level of sympathetic outflow to the sweat glands (T1–L2) results in anhidrosis below the injury level, with heatstroke risk proportional to the extent of denervated body surface.

Oxybutynin, amitriptyline, diphenhydramine, hyoscine, and related drugs block the M3 muscarinic receptor on eccrine gland cells; the risk is additive with multiple anticholinergics and is clinically significant in hot climates or during exercise.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex and X-linked ectodermal dysplasia
riskFactors.description: X-linked hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia almost exclusively affects hemizygous males; female carriers have patchy sweat gland distribution. Approximately 1 in 100,000 males are affected.

riskFactors.factor: Hot climate or occupational heat exposure
riskFactors.description: Environmental heat stress reveals the clinical consequence of anhidrosis; individuals with subclinical anhidrosis in temperate climates may only experience symptoms when exposed to a hot environment or vigorous exercise, making heat-related work a significant risk multiplier.

affectedDemographics: Adults with longstanding type 1 or type 2 diabetes with autonomic neuropathy
Patients with Parkinson disease, multiple system atrophy, or other synucleinopathies
Individuals with primary Sjögren syndrome or systemic lupus erythematosus
People with spinal cord injuries above the thoracic sympathetic outflow
Male infants and children with X-linked hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia
Older adults taking multiple anticholinergic medications
Young men with idiopathic acquired generalised anhidrosis (a rare but recognised distinct entity)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of anhidrosis requires confirming the absence or reduction of sweating and then identifying the level and cause of the defect. The thermoregulatory sweat test (TST) is the most comprehensive functional test: the patient is coated with a sweat-indicator powder (such as quinizarin or alizarin red), placed in a heated cabinet to raise core temperature by 1°C, and photographed; areas that do not change colour have absent sweating. The quantitative sudomotor axon reflex test (QSART) assesses post-ganglionic sympathetic function by applying acetylcholine iontophoretically and measuring the axon-reflex-mediated sweat response; reduced or absent QSART output localises the defect to post-ganglionic fibres. The silicone imprint method or standard evaporimetry can confirm anhidrosis in a specific body area. Skin punch biopsy with immunohistochemical staining for PGP9.5 (a pan-neuronal marker) quantifies intra-epidermal nerve fibre density and sweat gland innervation, distinguishing denervation from intrinsic gland loss. Serological tests for underlying causes include fasting glucose and HbA1c (diabetes), anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies (Sjögren), ANA, antinuclear antibodies, and autonomic function testing. MRI of the spinal cord is ordered when segmental or dermatomal anhidrosis is present. A thorough medication review is essential to exclude drug-induced anhidrosis before undertaking extensive investigation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the entire body surface for sweat gland function by coating skin with a temperature-sensitive indicator powder; areas that fail to colour in a warm environment are anhidrotic. Establishes the extent and distribution of anhidrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You are dusted with alizarin red or quinizarin powder and placed in a controlled-heat cabinet for 30–60 minutes while core temperature is monitored; sweat turns the powder from yellow to purple. Results are photographed. Takes about 90 minutes total.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures post-ganglionic sympathetic cholinergic function at standard sites (forearm, leg, foot) by stimulating axon reflexes with acetylcholine iontophoresis; reduced output distinguishes post-ganglionic denervation from central causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small electrode delivers a mild electrical current (iontophoresis of acetylcholine) to the skin for 5 minutes at each site; sweat output is measured by a capsule attached to the skin. Mild tingling at the stimulation site; no lasting discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies intra-epidermal nerve fibre density (IENFD) and sweat gland innervation using PGP9.5 staining; the definitive test to distinguish denervation from primary gland loss, and to diagnose small-fibre neuropathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3–4 mm punch biopsy is taken from the distal leg (and sometimes thigh for comparison) under local anaesthetic; small stitch or closure strip applied. Results in 1–2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for the most common systemic causes of acquired anhidrosis: Sjögren syndrome (anti-Ro, anti-La), general autoimmune disease (ANA), and diabetes (fasting glucose, HbA1c).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard fasting blood draw; panel results available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Autonomic function testing (tilt table, Valsalva manoeuvre, heart rate variability)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the overall integrity of the autonomic nervous system; widespread autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension, reduced heart rate variability) supports a systemic cause such as multiple system atrophy or diabetic autonomic neuropathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You are placed on a tilt table that moves from horizontal to upright; heart rate and blood pressure are monitored continuously. Valsalva testing involves sustained blowing against resistance. Takes 30–60 minutes; mild lightheadedness may occur.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects syringomyelia, demyelinating lesions, or structural cord compression causing segmental anhidrosis and provides anatomical mapping of the lesion level.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30–45 minute MRI scan; no injection required unless contrast enhancement is requested. Loud intermittent noise from the scanner; earplugs provided.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of anhidrosis is directed primarily at the underlying cause, with parallel strategies to prevent hyperthermia and protect patient safety. Where a reversible cause exists — drug-induced anhidrosis (stopping the anticholinergic agent), diabetic neuropathy (optimising glycaemic control), or autoimmune anhidrosis (immunosuppression) — addressing the root cause can restore some sweating function over months. For idiopathic acquired generalised anhidrosis (IAGA), corticosteroid courses at prednisolone 30–60 mg/day tapered over 4–8 weeks produce remission in approximately 40–60% of cases; relapse is common and may require repeat courses. Ciclosporin, intravenous immunoglobulin, and other immunosuppressants have produced benefit in steroid-refractory cases according to published case series. Oral pilocarpine (a cholinergic agonist, 5–10 mg two to three times daily) may partially stimulate residual eccrine gland function when innervation is preserved but signalling is impaired; side effects including salivation, gastrointestinal cramping, and sweating in unaffected areas limit the dose. For Sjögren-related anhidrosis, hydroxychloroquine or systemic immunosuppression targeting the underlying disease is the primary approach. The cornerstone of daily management in all forms is environmental and behavioural: avoiding exercise in hot conditions, pre-cooling the body before planned activity, wearing phase-change or evaporative cooling vests, scheduling outdoor tasks during cooler parts of the day, and maintaining immediate access to cold water, cold packs, and a cooling plan. Congenital anhidrosis requires lifelong temperature monitoring with particular vigilance during febrile illnesses, hot weather, and childhood physical activity. Patient education about heatstroke warning signs and emergency cooling protocols is as important as any pharmacological intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for idiopathic acquired generalised anhidrosis (IAGA) and autoimmune-mediated eccrine gland destruction, particularly in Sjögren-associated anhidrosis. A course of 30–60 mg/day tapered over 4–8 weeks can induce remission; maintenance with a low dose may be needed. Long-term risks include osteoporosis, hyperglycaemia, and adrenal suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Induces sweating recovery in approximately 40–60% of IAGA patients in published case series; remission duration varies from months to years.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral pilocarpine
medicalTreatments.description: A cholinergic agonist that directly stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors on eccrine gland cells; used at 5–10 mg twice or three times daily to partially compensate for impaired cholinergic signalling. Most useful when gland innervation is preserved but signalling is reduced. Side effects include salivation, sweating in unaffected areas, and gastrointestinal cramping.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increases stimulated sweat output by 20–40% in patients with partial eccrine dysfunction; data specific to anhidrosis are based mainly on Sjögren syndrome trials showing 61–65% response for xerostomia.

medicalTreatments.name: Strict glycaemic optimisation (for diabetic anhidrosis)
medicalTreatments.description: Reducing HbA1c toward individualised targets (typically <53–58 mmol/mol in adults without hypoglycaemia risk) slows progressive sympathetic nerve damage. Combination of insulin intensification, SGLT-2 inhibitors, and GLP-1 receptor agonists addresses both glucose and metabolic factors; improvement in autonomic neuropathy is modest but measurable over years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Intensive glucose control reduces the incidence of autonomic neuropathy by approximately 60% in type 1 diabetes (DCCT/EDIC trial) and slows progression in type 2 diabetes.

medicalTreatments.name: Ciclosporin or intravenous immunoglobulin (refractory IAGA)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line immunosuppression for patients who relapse after or fail to respond to corticosteroids. Small case series support benefit; no randomised controlled trials exist for anhidrosis specifically. Reserved for cases with significant heat intolerance or recurrent hyperthermia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case reports and small series report sweating restoration in approximately 30–50% of steroid-refractory IAGA patients with ciclosporin.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cooling vests and wearable cooling technology
alternativeTreatments.description: Phase-change or evaporative cooling vests that absorb or dissipate body heat independently of sweating; used before and during exercise or heat exposure. Reduces heat strain significantly in anhidrotic individuals, with studies in spinal cord injury patients showing core temperature reductions of 0.4–0.8°C during exercise.

alternativeTreatments.name: External misting and cold-water spraying
alternativeTreatments.description: Applying cold water to the skin surface mimics the evaporative cooling effect of sweat; regular misting of the face, neck, and exposed skin during heat exposure can delay hyperthermia by 10–20 minutes, providing a critical safety margin.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-cooling (cold-water immersion before exercise)
alternativeTreatments.description: Immersing the body or limbs in cold water for 10–15 minutes before exercise in the heat lowers pre-exercise core temperature and extends the time before a critical temperature is reached; used in competitive sport and rehabilitation for spinal cord injury patients with anhidrosis.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist evaluates sweat gland function using TST, QSART, or biopsy and manages autoimmune causes with skin-directed treatments. A neurologist is essential when peripheral neuropathy, Parkinson disease, multiple system atrophy, or spinal cord lesions are suspected. An endocrinologist guides management of diabetic autonomic neuropathy. Patients with generalised anhidrosis and significant heat intolerance — particularly those at occupational risk — should have a formal safety management plan developed by a specialist team.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a dermatologist or neurologist with access to autonomic function testing, QSART, and thermoregulatory sweat testing — typically found in academic medical centres or specialist autonomic clinics. For children with suspected congenital anhidrosis, a paediatric dermatologist or clinical geneticist with ectodermal dysplasia experience is the appropriate referral. Check whether the clinic can co-ordinate dermatological, neurological, and endocrinological evaluation in a single pathway, which reduces the diagnostic odyssey common in this condition.
preventionStrategies: Maintain tight glycaemic control if you have diabetes — intensive glucose management reduces the risk and slows progression of autonomic neuropathy affecting sweat glands.
Review all medications regularly for anticholinergic burden, especially in older adults; reduce or switch agents when heat exposure is anticipated.
Individuals diagnosed with anhidrosis should always carry a cooling plan and alert companions to the risk of rapid hyperthermia in warm environments.
Children with ectodermal dysplasia should have indoor temperature-controlled environments, cooling strategies at school, and an emergency hyperthermia protocol shared with teachers.
Patients with Sjögren syndrome or other autoimmune conditions should have sweat function checked periodically as part of multidisciplinary follow-up.
lifestyleModifications: Exercise exclusively in cool or air-conditioned environments; avoid outdoor exertion when ambient temperature exceeds 25°C if generalised anhidrosis is present.
Pre-cool the body with a cold shower or cold-water immersion for 10 minutes before any planned physical activity in warm conditions.
Wear cooling vests, ice packs under clothing, and lightweight, light-coloured, loose-fitting garments that maximise heat dissipation by conduction and radiation.
Carry a personal misting bottle and apply cold water to face, neck, and wrists every 5–10 minutes during any warm-weather activity.
Inform family members, employers, and exercise partners about the condition and the signs of heat exhaustion — rapid heartbeat, dizziness, confusion — so that they can act immediately.
Keep emergency cooling supplies (cold packs, cold water, a thermometer) at home, at work, and in vehicles.

recommended: Ample fluid intake — at least 2–3 litres of water daily, and more during exercise or heat exposure, since remaining heat-loss mechanisms (radiation, conduction) depend on adequate hydration
Electrolyte-containing drinks during prolonged heat exposure, since compensatory sweating elsewhere may cause sodium and potassium losses
Cold foods and drinks to help pre-cool the body and slow core temperature rise
A balanced diet supporting glycaemic control if diabetes is an underlying cause (whole grains, vegetables, lean protein, limited added sugar)
avoid: Alcohol, which promotes vasodilation and dehydration and reduces core temperature tolerance
Caffeine in excess during heat exposure, as it has mild diuretic effects
Hot, spicy foods and hot beverages during periods of heat exposure, which contribute to core temperature rise
Highly processed foods high in sodium that increase obligatory water requirements

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is safe and beneficial in people with anhidrosis when performed in a cool environment with appropriate cooling measures in place. Aquatic exercise (swimming, hydrotherapy) provides simultaneous cooling and is particularly well suited. Target environments at or below 18–20°C ambient temperature for aerobic activity. Monitor body temperature with a tympanic or rectal thermometer during extended exercise. Stop immediately if body temperature exceeds 38.5°C, if dizziness or confusion develops, or if the skin becomes dry and hot. Exercise rehabilitation for patients with spinal cord injury-related anhidrosis should be conducted at a centre familiar with autonomic dysreflexia and thermoregulation.
dailyManagement: Check the weather forecast every morning; plan activities to avoid peak heat hours (11:00–15:00) and have a contingency plan for unexpected heat.
Keep a thermometer at home and monitor body temperature during periods of exertion or on hot days.
Ensure air conditioning at home and at work is functional before the summer season; register with local emergency services if available for priority cooling shelter access.
Report new or progressive anhidrosis promptly to a clinician — expanding areas of lost sweating in a diabetic patient may signal worsening neuropathy requiring treatment adjustment.
Carry a medical alert card or wear a bracelet stating 'anhidrosis — risk of heatstroke' so that emergency responders can act appropriately if hyperthermia occurs away from home.
prognosis: The prognosis of anhidrosis depends entirely on its cause. Drug-induced anhidrosis resolves fully when the offending medication is stopped. Idiopathic acquired generalised anhidrosis treated with corticosteroids achieves durable remission in approximately 40–60% of cases. Diabetic autonomic neuropathy-related anhidrosis generally progresses slowly but can be stabilised with optimal glycaemic control; it rarely reverses significantly. Parkinson disease and multiple system atrophy-associated anhidrosis worsens with disease progression and does not meaningfully improve with current therapies; heat management is lifelong. Congenital anhidrosis from ectodermal dysplasia is a permanent condition; with appropriate environmental management and cooling strategies, affected individuals can lead full lives with an acceptable quality of life. The most significant adverse outcome is heat-related illness: hospitalised heatstroke carries a mortality of 10–60% depending on the patient population, making prevention the critical priority.
recoveryTimeline: When a reversible cause is addressed, improvement in sweating can be detected by QSART or TST within 4–8 weeks of starting corticosteroids or stopping the offending drug. Full restoration of sweating — if it occurs — takes 3–6 months. In diabetic neuropathy, even optimal glucose control produces only gradual partial improvement over 12–24 months. For congenital and progressive neurological causes, no recovery timeline applies — the focus is on adaptation and lifelong heat avoidance rather than recovery of function.
complications: Heatstroke — the most serious complication; generalised anhidrosis in hot conditions can produce core temperatures above 40°C within 30–60 minutes of exercise, leading to multi-organ failure with a fatality rate of 10–60% if untreated.

Heat exhaustion — a milder but clinically significant heat-related illness causing weakness, nausea, headache, and collapse; requires immediate cooling and fluid replacement and can progress to heatstroke if not addressed.

Miliaria rubra (prickly heat) — eccrine duct occlusion in patients with partial anhidrosis produces an intensely itchy erythematous rash as backed-up eccrine secretion leaks into the dermis; further impairs what residual sweating capacity remains.

Psychological impact — heat intolerance limits outdoor activity, work, exercise, and social participation; anxiety about heat exposure, depression, and occupational restrictions are documented sequelae.

Progressive neuropathy — in diabetic or autoimmune anhidrosis, the same process damaging sweat gland innervation also causes orthostatic hypotension, gastroparesis, and urinary dysfunction, compounding morbidity.

supportResources.name: National Foundation for Ectodermal Dysplasias (NFED)
supportResources.description: Dedicated support and educational resources for people with congenital anhidrosis from ectodermal dysplasia, including school guidance, medical protocols, and peer networks.

supportResources.name: Sjögren's Foundation — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Support and information for patients with primary Sjögren syndrome, including guidance on glandular complications such as anhidrosis.

supportResources.name: American Diabetes Association — Autonomic Neuropathy
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information from the ADA on diabetic autonomic neuropathy, a leading cause of acquired anhidrosis.

sources.title: American Diabetes Association — Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes 2022 (Autonomic Neuropathy)
Low PA. Evaluation of sudomotor function. Clinical Neurophysiology, 2004
Sato KT et al. Anhidrosis: a review of the anatomy, physiology and clinical relevance. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology, 2003
Nakazato Y et al. Idiopathic pure sudomotor failure: anhidrosis due to deficits in cholinergic transmission. Neurology, 2004
Ziemssen T, Reichmann H. Cardiovascular autonomic dysfunction in Parkinson's disease. Journal of the Neurological Sciences, 2010
Brandt T et al. Disorders of the autonomic nervous system. In: Brandt T et al., eds. Neurological Disorders: Course and Treatment, 2003
Casa DJ et al. National Athletic Trainers' Association Position Statement: Exertional Heat Illnesses. Journal of Athletic Training, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalised hyperhidrosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyperhidrosis is excess sweating — the functional opposite of anhidrosis. Patients sometimes describe the absence of sweating as 'unusual dryness' while a sweating disorder is suspected; the thermoregulatory sweat test clearly distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary focal hyperhidrosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Focal hyperhidrosis causes localised excess sweating (armpits, palms, feet) driven by an overactive cholinergic reflex, whereas anhidrosis is the complete absence of sweating; patients with compensatory hyperhidrosis in anhidrosis may initially be assessed for hyperhidrosis in the sweating zone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sjögren syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sjögren syndrome causes dry eyes and dry mouth as its primary features and may be mistaken for localised dryness rather than recognised as a systemic exocrinopathy that can also destroy eccrine glands. Anhidrosis in Sjögren is often unrecognised until autonomic testing is performed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ichthyosis vulgaris
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ichthyosis vulgaris causes dry, scaly skin that can reduce sweating by blocking eccrine duct openings; it is distinguished from primary anhidrosis by the characteristic fish-scale skin appearance, a positive family history, and filaggrin gene mutations.

relatedConditions.name: Generalised hyperhidrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The functional opposite of anhidrosis, involving the same eccrine gland system; compensatory hyperhidrosis above the anhidrotic zone is a clinical marker of level of the autonomic lesion.

relatedConditions.name: Anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: The leading congenital cause of anhidrosis, characterised by absent eccrine glands from birth alongside sparse hair and abnormal teeth.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetic autonomic neuropathy is one of the most common acquired causes of anhidrosis; sweat gland innervation loss is a length-dependent, distal-to-proximal process.

relatedConditions.name: Horner syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Facial anhidrosis is part of the classic Horner triad (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), reflecting interruption of the ipsilateral cervical sympathetic chain.

relatedConditions.name: Parkinson disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Alpha-synuclein-driven autonomic degeneration in Parkinson disease damages sympathetic sweat gland innervation, producing patchy or generalised anhidrosis in up to 50% of patients.

faqs.question: What is anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Anhidrosis is the inability to sweat normally. Since sweating is the main way the human body cools itself, anhidrosis creates a serious risk of overheating during exercise or in hot weather. It may affect the whole body or only one area, and can result from nerve damage, autoimmune disease, diabetes, medications, or a congenital condition.

faqs.question: What causes anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: The most common acquired causes are diabetic autonomic neuropathy, primary Sjögren syndrome, idiopathic acquired generalised anhidrosis, spinal cord injury, Parkinson disease, and anticholinergic medications. Congenital anhidrosis results from absent eccrine glands in X-linked hypohidrotic ectodermal dysplasia. A medication review is the first step before extensive investigation.

faqs.question: Is anhidrosis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes, potentially life-threatening. Without sweating, the body cannot cool itself effectively, and core temperature can rise above 40°C during exercise or heat exposure, causing heatstroke. Heatstroke carries a mortality rate of 10–60% in hospitalised patients if not rapidly treated. Patients with anhidrosis must have a strict heat avoidance and cooling plan.

faqs.question: How is anhidrosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A thermoregulatory sweat test maps which areas of the body sweat; QSART testing assesses post-ganglionic nerve function; and skin biopsy with PGP9.5 staining quantifies sweat gland innervation. Blood tests screen for diabetes, Sjögren syndrome, and other autoimmune causes. A medication review is always part of the workup.

faqs.question: Can anhidrosis be treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. Drug-induced anhidrosis resolves when the medication is stopped. Idiopathic acquired anhidrosis responds to corticosteroids in about 40–60% of cases. Diabetic anhidrosis is slowed but not reversed by better glucose control. Congenital anhidrosis is permanent and managed through lifelong heat avoidance and cooling strategies.

faqs.question: What is the difference between anhidrosis and hypohidrosis?
faqs.answer: Anhidrosis is complete or near-complete absence of sweating. Hypohidrosis is reduced but not absent sweating. The distinction is important because hypohidrosis still provides some cooling capacity, while anhidrosis eliminates it. Both are diagnosed on the same sweat tests, and the terms are sometimes used interchangeably in clinical practice.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diabetic autonomic neuropathy damages the sympathetic nerve fibres that stimulate eccrine glands, causing distal anhidrosis that progresses proximally over years. It is one of the most common causes of acquired anhidrosis. Tight glucose control slows progression but rarely reverses established nerve damage.

faqs.question: What medications cause anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Anticholinergic drugs block the muscarinic receptors that trigger sweating. The main culprits are oxybutynin (for bladder urgency), tricyclic antidepressants, antihistamines, scopolamine patches, and some antipsychotics. The risk is additive if multiple anticholinergics are taken together and is higher in older adults. Stopping or switching the drug usually restores sweating.

faqs.question: How do I stay safe with anhidrosis in hot weather?
faqs.answer: Exercise only in air-conditioned environments or during the coolest parts of the day. Pre-cool your body with a cold shower before activity. Wear a cooling vest, use a misting spray on exposed skin, monitor your body temperature, and stop activity immediately if you feel dizzy or overheated. Always carry cold water and inform companions of your condition.

faqs.question: What is idiopathic acquired generalised anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: IAGA is a sudden-onset loss of sweating across the whole body in otherwise healthy people, most often young men. The exact cause is unclear but may involve autoimmune attack on post-ganglionic cholinergic nerve fibres. Corticosteroids produce remission in about 40–60% of patients; relapse is common but treatable with repeated courses.

faqs.question: Can anhidrosis affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes. The most common form in children is anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (X-linked), in which eccrine glands are absent from birth. Affected babies develop unexplained fevers in warm weather or during illness. Once diagnosed, temperature monitoring and cooling protocols prevent dangerous hyperthermia.

faqs.question: What is compensatory hyperhidrosis in anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: When anhidrosis affects one part of the body, the intact sweating areas work harder to compensate, producing excessive sweating in those regions — for example, profuse facial and head sweating in a patient with distal lower-body anhidrosis. Compensatory hyperhidrosis can itself be uncomfortable but is a useful clinical sign pointing to a focal lesion level.

faqs.question: Does Parkinson disease cause anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Parkinson disease causes progressive degeneration of autonomic ganglia and peripheral sympathetic fibres, reducing sweat gland innervation in up to 50% of patients compared with age-matched controls. Multiple system atrophy — a related condition — causes more severe and generalised anhidrosis. Heat management becomes increasingly important as the disease progresses.

faqs.question: What is the thermoregulatory sweat test?
faqs.answer: The thermoregulatory sweat test (TST) is the standard test to map anhidrosis across the whole body. You are coated with a heat-sensitive indicator powder and placed in a heated cabinet; areas that sweat change the powder colour. Areas that do not change colour are anhidrotic. It takes about 90 minutes and is performed in specialist autonomic or dermatology centres.

faqs.question: Can anhidrosis cause prickly heat?
faqs.answer: Yes. When eccrine ducts are blocked — a common finding in partial anhidrosis — backed-up sweat leaks into the dermis and causes miliaria rubra, the intensely itchy red rash known as prickly heat. This paradoxically worsens the effective anhidrosis by further reducing sweat delivery to the skin surface.

faqs.question: Is anhidrosis an autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: In some cases, yes. Primary Sjögren syndrome and some cases of idiopathic acquired generalised anhidrosis involve immune-mediated destruction of eccrine gland cells or their nerve supply. These forms may respond to immunosuppressive treatment. Other causes of anhidrosis — such as diabetes or spinal cord injury — are not autoimmune.

faqs.question: What does anhidrosis feel like?
faqs.answer: People with anhidrosis typically notice that their skin stays completely dry in hot conditions or during exercise when others around them are sweating heavily. They feel overheated, uncomfortable, and flushed much sooner than expected. Some develop headaches or dizziness on hot days, and the skin in the affected area may feel hot, dry, and tight.

faqs.question: Can anhidrosis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Drug-induced anhidrosis resolves when the medication is stopped. Some cases of idiopathic acquired anhidrosis remit spontaneously. Most other forms — particularly those from diabetic neuropathy, Parkinson disease, or congenital gland absence — are persistent without treatment. Always seek evaluation rather than waiting, given the heatstroke risk.

faqs.question: Is sweating necessary for health?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sweating is the human body's primary mechanism for dissipating heat during exercise and hot weather. Without it, core temperature rises rapidly. Anhidrosis is therefore a medically significant impairment of thermoregulation, not merely a cosmetic issue.

faqs.question: What specialist treats anhidrosis?
faqs.answer: A dermatologist can confirm the diagnosis and assess eccrine gland function with sweat tests and biopsy. A neurologist evaluates the autonomic nervous system and underlying neurological causes. An endocrinologist manages diabetic autonomic neuropathy. For complex cases, a specialist autonomic clinic co-ordinating all three disciplines provides the most comprehensive evaluation.

metaDescription: Anhidrosis is the inability to sweat normally, causing dangerous overheating. Learn about diabetes-linked causes, diagnosis, and how to stay safe in heat.
